pairs.question: What titles were created for John Drummond in the Peerage of Scotland on 14 April 1685?
pairs.answer: On 14 April 1685, the titles of Viscount of Melfort and Lord Drummond of Gillestoun were created in the Peerage of Scotland for John Drummond, second son of James Drummond, 3rd Earl of Perth. These titles were granted with remainder to the heirs male of his body by his second marriage to Euphemia Wallace, and failing that, to his other male heirs.

pairs.question: How did John Drummond become the Earl of Melfort, and what was the remainder clause for his titles?
pairs.answer: John Drummond was further created Earl of Melfort, along with the titles Viscount of Forth and Lord Drummond of Riccartoun, Castlemains, and Gilstoun, in the Peerage of Scotland on 12 August 1686. The remainder clause for these titles was similar to the 1685 grant, passing to the heirs male of his body by his second marriage to Euphemia Wallace, and failing that, to his other male heirs.

pairs.question: What Jacobite titles were granted to John Drummond (Duke of Melfort) by King James II while in exile?
pairs.answer: While in exile in France, King James II granted John Drummond several Jacobite titles: Baron Cleworth in the Jacobite Peerage of England (7 August 1689), and Duke of Melfort, Marquess of Forth, Earl of Isla and Burntisland, Viscount of Rickerton, and Lord Castlemains and Galston in the Jacobite Peerage of Scotland (17 April 1692). All these titles were granted with a similar remainder to the 1685 viscountcy.

pairs.question: Why and when were the titles of the Earl of Melfort attainted by the British Parliament?
pairs.answer: The titles of the Earl of Melfort were attainted by Act of Parliament on 2 July 1695 due to John Drummond's support for the deposed King James II and his involvement in the Jacobite cause. This followed his outlawry by the de facto régime in England on 23 July 1694. As a result, his honours became forfeit.

pairs.question: What happened to the French peerage titles of the Duke of Melfort after his attainder in Britain?
pairs.answer: After his attainder in Britain, the Duke of Melfort was granted the honours and precedence of a French peer by Louis XIV in 1701. He and his descendants thereafter used the title 'Duc de Melfort' in France, though this was a French translation of their Jacobite dukedom and not a recognized French dukedom. The British government never acknowledged these French titles.

pairs.question: How did the 4th Duke of Melfort (titular) succeed to the Earldom of Perth in 1800?
pairs.answer: On 2 July 1800, the titular 4th Duke of Melfort succeeded as heir male to the attainted Earldom of Perth upon the death of James Drummond, Lord Perth and Baron Drummond of Stobhall. This connection was due to his descent from the 1st Earl and Duke of Melfort through his first marriage to Sophie Maitland.

pairs.question: What legal steps did the 6th Duke of Melfort (titular) take to reclaim his French titles, and when?
pairs.answer: In 1841, the titular 6th Duke of Melfort established his right to the French titles of Duc de Melfort, Comte de Lussan, and Baron de Valrose before the French Council of State and the Tribunal de la Seine. His claim was based on his great-grandfather, the 2nd Duke of Melfort, who had married Marie Gabrielle d'Audebert, daughter of Jean d'Audebert, Comte de Lussan, in 1707.

pairs.question: How did the attainder of the Melfort titles eventually get reversed in Britain?
pairs.answer: The attainder of the Melfort titles was reversed on 28 June 1853, allowing the titular 6th Duke of Melfort to become the 5th Earl of Perth and reclaim other ancestral titles. This followed his successful proof of descent from the 1st Earl of Melfort to the incumbent British authorities in 1848.
